metadata.title: Dostoevsky: Better Space-Time Trade-Offs for LSM-Tree Based Key-Value Stores via Adaptive Removal of Superfluous Merging
metadata.titleTranslation: Dostoevsky：通过自适应移除多余合并，为基于 LSM 树的键值存储实现更优的时空权衡
metadata.source: Dayan和Idreos - 2018 - Dostoevsky Better Space-Time Trade-Offs for LSM-Tree Based Key-Value Stores via Adaptive Removal of(4).pdf

sections.title: Abstract
sections.originalMarkdown:
We show that all mainstream LSM-tree based key-value stores in the literature and in industry suboptimally trade between the I/O cost of updates on one hand and the I/O cost of lookups and storage space on the other. The reason is that they perform equally expensive merge operations across all levels of LSM-tree to bound the number of runs that a lookup has to probe and to remove obsolete entries to reclaim storage space. With state-of-the-art designs, however, merge operations from all levels of LSM-tree but the largest (i.e., most merge operations) reduce point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and storage space by a negligible amount while significantly adding to the amortized cost of updates.

To address this problem, we introduce Lazy Leveling, a new design that removes merge operations from all levels of LSM-tree but the largest. Lazy Leveling improves the worst-case complexity of update cost while maintaining the same bounds on point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and storage space. We further introduce Fluid LSM-tree, a generalization of the entire LSM-tree design space that can be parameterized to assume any existing design. Relative to Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-tree can optimize more for updates by merging less at the largest level, or it can optimize more for short range lookups by merging more at all other levels.

We put everything together to design Dostoevsky, a key-value store that adaptively removes superfluous merging by navigating the Fluid LSM-tree design space based on the application workload and hardware. We implemented Dostoevsky on top of RocksDB, and we show that it strictly dominates state-of-the-art designs in terms of performance and storage space.
sections.translationMarkdown:
我们证明，文献界和工业界所有主流的基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储，在一方面的更新 I/O 成本与另一方面的查询 I/O 成本和存储空间之间所作的权衡都不是最优的。原因在于，它们在 LSM-tree 的所有层执行成本同样高昂的合并操作，以限制查询必须探查的sorted run数量，并删除过时条目来回收存储空间。然而，在最先进的设计中，除最大层之外所有层的合并操作（即绝大多数合并操作），对点查询成本、长范围查询成本和存储空间的降低都微乎其微，却显著增加了更新的摊销成本。

为解决这一问题，我们提出 Lazy Leveling，这是一种新设计，它移除了 LSM-tree 中除最大层之外所有层的合并操作。Lazy Leveling 改善了更新成本的最坏情况复杂度，同时维持点查询成本、长范围查询成本和存储空间的相同界限。我们进一步提出 Fluid LSM-tree，它是对整个 LSM-tree 设计空间的泛化，可以通过参数化采用任何现有设计。相较于 Lazy Leveling，Fluid LSM-tree 可以通过减少最大层的合并来进一步优化更新，也可以通过增加其他所有层的合并来进一步优化短范围查询。

我们将上述思想结合起来，设计了 Dostoevsky：一种根据应用工作负载和硬件在 Fluid LSM-tree 设计空间中进行导航，从而自适应地移除多余合并的键值存储。我们在 RocksDB 之上实现了 Dostoevsky，并证明它在性能和存储空间方面严格支配最先进的设计。

sections.title: 1 INTRODUCTION
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Key-Value Stores and LSM-Trees.** A key-value store is a database that efficiently maps from search keys to their corresponding data values. Key-value stores are used everywhere today from graph processing in social media [8, 17] to event log processing in cyber security [18] to online transaction processing [27]. To persist key-value entries in storage, most key-value stores today use LSM-tree [41]. LSM-tree buffers inserted/updated entries in main memory and flushes the buffer as a sorted run to secondary storage every time that it fills up. LSM-tree later sort-merges these runs to bound the number of runs that a lookup has to probe and to remove obsolete entries, i.e., for which there exists a more recent entry with the same key. LSM-tree organizes runs into levels of exponentially increasing capacities whereby larger levels contain older runs. As entries are updated out-of-place, a point lookup finds the most recent version of an entry by probing the levels from smallest to largest and terminating when it finds the target key. A range lookup, on the other hand, has to access the relevant key range from across all runs at all levels and to eliminate obsolete entries from the result set. To speed up lookups on individual runs, modern designs maintain two additional structures in main memory. First, for every run there is a set of fence pointers that contain the first key of every block of the run; this allows lookups to access a particular key within a run with just one I/O. Second, for every run there exists a Bloom filter; this allows point lookups to skip runs that do not contain the target key. This overall design is adopted in a large number of modern key-value stores including LevelDB [32] and BigTable [19] at Google, RocksDB [29] at Facebook, Cassandra [34], HBase [7] and Accumulo [5] at Apache, Voldemort [38] at LinkedIn, Dynamo [26] at Amazon, WiredTiger [52] at MongoDB, and bLSM [48] and cLSM [31] at Yahoo. Relational databases today such as MySQL (using MyRocks [28]) and SQLite4 support this design too as a storage engine by mapping primary keys to rows as values.

*Figure 1: Dostoevsky enables richer space-time trade-offs among updates, point lookups, range lookups and space-amplification, and it navigates the design space to find the best trade-off for a particular application.*

**The Problem.** The frequency of merge operations in LSM-tree controls an intrinsic trade-off between the I/O cost of updates on one hand and the I/O cost of lookups and storage space-amplification (i.e., caused by the presence of obsolete entries) on the other. The problem is that existing designs trade suboptimally among these metrics. Figure 1 conceptually depicts this by plotting point lookup cost and space-amplification on the y-axis against update cost on the x-axis (while these y-axis metrics have different units, their trade-off curves with respect to the x-axis have the same shape). The two points at the edges of the curves are a log and a sorted array. LSM-tree degenerates into these edge points when it does not merge at all or when it merges as much as possible, respectively. We place mainstream systems along the top curve between these edge points based on their default merge frequencies, and we draw a superior trade-off curve for Monkey [22], which represents the current state of the art. We show that there exists an even superior trade-off curve to Monkey. Existing designs forgo a significant amount of performance and/or storage space for not being designed along this bottom curve.

**The Problem’s Source.** By analyzing the design space of state-of-the-art LSM-trees, we pinpoint the problem to the fact that the worst-case update cost, point lookup cost, range lookup cost, and space-amplification derive differently from across different levels.

- **Updates.** The I/O cost of an update is paid later through the merge operations that the updated entry participates in. While merge operations at larger levels entail exponentially more work, they take place exponentially less frequently. Therefore, updates derive their I/O cost equally from merge operations across all levels.
- **Point lookups.** While mainstream designs along the top curve in Figure 1 set the same false positive rate to Bloom filters across all levels of LSM-tree, Monkey, the current state of the art, sets exponentially lower false positive rates to Bloom filters at smaller levels [22]. This is shown to minimize the sum of false positive rates across all filters and to thereby minimize I/O for point lookups. At the same time, this means that access to smaller levels is exponentially less probable. Therefore, most point lookup I/Os target the largest level.
- **Long range lookups¹.** As levels in LSM-tree have exponentially increasing capacities, the largest level contains most of the data, and so it tends to contain most of the entries within a given key-range. Therefore, most I/Os issued by long range lookups target the largest level.
- **Short range lookups.** Range lookups with extremely small key ranges only access approximately one block within each run regardless of the run’s size. As the maximum number of runs per level is fixed in state-of-the-art designs, short range lookups derive their I/O cost equally from across all levels.
- **Space-Amplification.** The worst-case space-amplification occurs when all entries at smaller levels are updates to entries at the largest level. Therefore, the highest fraction of obsolete entries in the worst-case is at the largest level.

Since the worst-case point lookup cost, long range lookup cost and space-amplification derive mostly from the largest level, merge operations at all levels of LSM-tree but the largest (i.e., most merge operations) hardly improve on these metrics while significantly adding to the amortized cost of updates. This leads to suboptimal trade-offs. We solve this problem from the ground up in three steps.

¹ In Section 3, we distinguish formally between short and long range lookups.

**Solution 1: Lazy Leveling to Remove Superfluous Merging.** We expand the LSM-tree design space with Lazy Leveling, a new design that removes merging from all but the largest level of LSM-tree. Lazy Leveling improves the worst-case cost complexity of updates while maintaining the same bounds on point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and space-amplification and while providing a competitive bound on short range lookup cost. We show that the improved update cost can be traded to reduce point lookup cost and space-amplification. This generates the bottom curve in Figure 1, which offers richer space-time trade-offs that have been impossible to achieve with state-of-the-art designs until now.

**Solution 2: Fluid LSM-Tree for Design Space Fluidity.** We introduce Fluid LSM-tree as a generalization of LSM-tree that enables transitioning fluidly across the whole LSM-tree design space. Fluid LSM-tree does this by controlling the frequency of merge operations separately for the largest level and for all other levels. Relative to Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-tree can optimize more for updates by merging less at the largest level, or it can optimize more for short range lookups by merging more at all other levels.

**Solution 3: Dostoevsky to Navigate the Design Space.** We put everything together in Dostoevsky: Space-Time Optimized Evolvable Scalable Key-Value Store. Dostoevsky analytically finds the tuning of Fluid LSM-tree that maximizes throughput for a particular application workload and hardware subject to a user constraint on space-amplification. It does this by pruning the search space to quickly find the best tuning and physically adapting to it during runtime. Since Dostoevsky spans all existing designs and is able to navigate to the best one for a given application, it strictly dominates existing key-value stores in terms of performance and space-amplification. We depict Dostoevsky in Figure 1 as a black star that can navigate the entire design space.

**Contributions.** Our contributions are summarized below.

- We show that state-of-the-art LSM-trees perform equally expensive merge operations across all levels of LSM-tree, yet merge operations at all but the largest level (i.e., most merge operations) improve point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and space-amplification by a negligible amount while adding significantly to the amortized cost of updates.
- We introduce Lazy Leveling to remove merge operations at all but the largest level. This improves the cost complexity of updates while maintaining the same bounds on point lookups, long range lookups, and space-amplification and while providing a competitive bound on short range lookups.
- We introduce Fluid LSM-tree, a generalization of LSM-tree that spans all existing designs. Relative to Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-tree can optimize more for updates by merging less at the largest level, or it can optimize more for short range lookups by merging more at all other levels.
- We introduce Dostoevsky, a key-value store that dynamically adapts across the Fluid LSM-tree design space to the design that maximizes the worst-case throughput based on the application workload and the hardware subject to a constraint on space-amplification.
- We implemented Dostoevsky on RocksDB and show that it dominates existing designs for any application scenario.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**键值存储与 LSM-tree。** 键值存储是一种能够高效地将搜索键映射到相应数据值的数据库。如今，键值存储应用广泛，从社交媒体中的图处理 [8, 17]，到网络安全中的事件日志处理 [18]，再到在线事务处理 [27]。为了将键值条目持久化到存储设备中，当今大多数键值存储都采用 LSM-tree [41]。LSM-tree 在主存中缓冲插入或更新的条目，并在缓冲区每次填满时将其作为一个sorted run刷写到二级存储。随后，LSM-tree 对这些sorted run进行排序合并，以限制一次查询必须探查的sorted run数量，并删除过时条目，即存在具有相同键且版本更新的条目。LSM-tree 将sorted run组织成容量呈指数增长的若干层，其中更大的层包含更旧的sorted run。由于条目采用异地更新，点查询会从最小层到最大层依次探查，并在找到目标键时终止，以此找到条目的最新版本。另一方面，范围查询必须访问所有层的所有sorted run中的相关键范围，并从结果集中消除过时条目。为了加速对单个sorted run的查询，现代设计在主存中维护两个额外结构。首先，每个sorted run都有一组围栏指针，其中包含该sorted run每个块的第一个键；这使查询只需一次 I/O 就能访问sorted run中的特定键。其次，每个sorted run都有一个 Bloom 过滤器；它使点查询能够跳过不包含目标键的sorted run。大量现代键值存储都采用了这一整体设计，包括 Google 的 LevelDB [32] 和 BigTable [19]、Facebook 的 RocksDB [29]、Apache 的 Cassandra [34]、HBase [7] 和 Accumulo [5]、LinkedIn 的 Voldemort [38]、Amazon 的 Dynamo [26]、MongoDB 的 WiredTiger [52]，以及 Yahoo 的 bLSM [48] 和 cLSM [31]。如今，MySQL（使用 MyRocks [28]）和 SQLite4 等关系数据库也支持将这一设计用作存储引擎，把主键映射到作为值的行。

*图 1：Dostoevsky 在更新、点查询、范围查询和空间放大之间实现了更丰富的时空权衡，并在设计空间中进行导航，从而为特定应用找到最佳权衡。*

**问题。** LSM-tree 中合并操作的频率控制着一种内在权衡：一方面是更新的 I/O 成本，另一方面是查询的 I/O 成本和存储空间放大（即由过时条目的存在所引起）。问题在于，现有设计在这些指标之间作出的权衡并非最优。图 1 对此进行了概念性描绘：纵轴是点查询成本和空间放大，横轴是更新成本（虽然纵轴上的这些指标具有不同单位，但它们相对于横轴的权衡曲线形状相同）。曲线两端的两个点分别是日志和有序数组。当 LSM-tree 完全不合并或尽可能多地合并时，它分别退化为这两个端点。我们根据默认合并频率，将主流系统放置在两个端点之间的上方曲线上，并为代表当前最先进水平的 Monkey [22] 绘制了一条更优的权衡曲线。我们证明，还存在一条比 Monkey 更优的权衡曲线。由于没有沿着这条底部曲线进行设计，现有设计放弃了大量性能和/或存储空间。

**问题的根源。** 通过分析最先进 LSM-tree 的设计空间，我们将问题定位为：最坏情况下的更新成本、点查询成本、范围查询成本和空间放大来自不同层的方式各不相同。

- **更新。** 一次更新的 I/O 成本在之后由更新条目所参与的合并操作支付。尽管更大层的合并操作需要指数级增加的工作量，但其发生频率也呈指数级降低。因此，更新的 I/O 成本均等地来自所有层的合并操作。
- **点查询。** 图 1 上方曲线中的主流设计为 LSM-tree 所有层的 Bloom 过滤器设置相同的假阳性率，而当前最先进的 Monkey 则为较小层的 Bloom 过滤器设置指数级更低的假阳性率 [22]。已有研究证明，这会使所有过滤器的假阳性率之和最小，从而使点查询的 I/O 最小。与此同时，这意味着访问较小层的概率呈指数级降低。因此，大多数点查询 I/O 都以最大层为目标。
- **长范围查询¹。** 由于 LSM-tree 各层容量呈指数增长，最大层包含绝大多数数据，因而往往也包含给定键范围内的大多数条目。因此，长范围查询发出的大多数 I/O 都以最大层为目标。
- **短范围查询。** 对于键范围极小的范围查询，无论sorted run大小如何，都只会访问每个sorted run中的大约一个块。由于最先进的设计中每层的最大sorted run数量是固定的，因此短范围查询的 I/O 成本均等地来自所有层。
- **空间放大。** 当较小层中的所有条目都是对最大层中条目的更新时，会出现最坏情况下的空间放大。因此，在最坏情况下，最大层中过时条目的比例最高。

由于最坏情况下的点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大主要来自最大层，因此，LSM-tree 中除最大层之外所有层的合并操作（即绝大多数合并操作）几乎不能改善这些指标，却显著增加了更新的摊销成本。这导致了次优的权衡。我们分三步从根本上解决这一问题。

¹ 在第 3 节中，我们对短范围查询和长范围查询作出形式化区分。

**解决方案 1：使用 Lazy Leveling 移除多余合并。** 我们以 Lazy Leveling 扩展 LSM-tree 的设计空间。Lazy Leveling 是一种新设计，它移除了 LSM-tree 中除最大层之外的所有合并。Lazy Leveling 改善了更新的最坏情况成本复杂度，同时维持点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大的相同界限，并为短范围查询提供有竞争力的界限。我们证明，可以用改善后的更新成本换取点查询成本和空间放大的降低。由此生成图 1 中的底部曲线，它提供了更丰富的时空权衡，而这是此前最先进的设计无法实现的。

**解决方案 2：使用 Fluid LSM-Tree 实现设计空间流动性。** 我们提出 Fluid LSM-tree，作为 LSM-tree 的一种泛化，使系统能够在整个 LSM-tree 设计空间中流畅转换。Fluid LSM-tree 通过分别控制最大层与其他所有层的合并操作频率来实现这一点。相较于 Lazy Leveling，Fluid LSM-tree 可以通过减少最大层的合并来进一步优化更新，也可以通过增加其他所有层的合并来进一步优化短范围查询。

**解决方案 3：使用 Dostoevsky 在设计空间中导航。** 我们将所有内容结合到 Dostoevsky 中，其全称为“时空优化、可演化、可扩展键值存储”（Space-Time Optimized Evolvable Scalable Key-Value Store）。在用户为空间放大设置约束的前提下，Dostoevsky 通过分析找到 Fluid LSM-tree 的参数配置，使特定应用工作负载和硬件上的吞吐量最大化。它通过剪枝搜索空间来快速找到最佳配置，并在运行期间以物理方式适应这一配置。由于 Dostoevsky 覆盖所有现有设计，并能针对给定应用导航至最佳设计，因此，它在性能和空间放大方面严格支配现有键值存储。在图 1 中，我们将 Dostoevsky 描绘为一颗能够在整个设计空间中导航的黑色星标。

**贡献。** 我们的贡献概括如下。

- 我们证明，最先进的 LSM-tree 在所有层执行成本同样高昂的合并操作，但除最大层之外所有层的合并操作（即绝大多数合并操作），对点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大的改善都微乎其微，却显著增加了更新的摊销成本。
- 我们提出 Lazy Leveling，以移除除最大层之外所有层的合并操作。这改善了更新的成本复杂度，同时维持点查询、长范围查询和空间放大的相同界限，并为短范围查询提供有竞争力的界限。
- 我们提出 Fluid LSM-tree，它是覆盖所有现有设计的 LSM-tree 泛化。相较于 Lazy Leveling，Fluid LSM-tree 可以通过减少最大层的合并来进一步优化更新，也可以通过增加其他所有层的合并来进一步优化短范围查询。
- 我们提出 Dostoevsky，这是一种键值存储，它能够在 Fluid LSM-tree 设计空间中动态适应某种设计；在空间放大约束下，该设计根据应用工作负载和硬件使最坏情况下的吞吐量最大化。
- 我们在 RocksDB 上实现了 Dostoevsky，并证明它在任何应用场景下都支配现有设计。

sections.title: 2 BACKGROUND
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section gives the necessary background on LSM-tree based key-value stores. Figure 2 illustrates their architecture and Table 1 gives a list of terms we use throughout the paper.

*Figure 2: An overview of an LSM-tree using the tiering and leveling merge policies.*

| Term | Definition | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | total number of entries | entries |
| $L$ | number of levels | levels |
| $L_{max}$ | maximum possible number of levels | levels |
| $B$ | number of entries that fit into a storage block | entries |
| $P$ | size of the buffer in storage blocks | blocks |
| $T$ | size ratio between adjacent levels | |
| $T_{lim}$ | size ratio at which $L$ converges to 1 | |
| $M$ | main memory allocated to the Bloom filters | bits |
| $p_i$ | false positive rate for Bloom filters at Level $i$ | |
| $s$ | selectivity of a range lookup | entries |
| $R$ | zero-result point lookup cost | I/Os |
| $V$ | non-zero-result point lookup cost | I/Os |
| $Q$ | range lookup cost | I/Os |
| $W$ | update cost | I/Os |
| $K$ | bound on number of runs at Levels 1 to $L - 1$ | runs |
| $Z$ | bound on number of runs at Level $L$ | runs |
| $μ$ | storage sequential over random access speed | |
| $ϕ$ | storage write over read speed | |

*Table 1: Table of terms used throughput the paper.*

**LSM-Tree Structure.** LSM-tree optimizes for write-heavy workloads. This is an important performance goal for systems today because the proportion of application writes is continuously increasing (e.g., in 2012 Yahoo! reported that the proportion of writes targeting their web-service was 50% and projected to continue accelerating [48]). To optimize for writes, LSM-tree initially buffers all updates, insertions, and deletes (henceforth referred to as updates unless otherwise mentioned) in main memory, as shown in Figure 2. When the buffer fills up, LSM-tree flushes the buffer to secondary storage as a sorted run. LSM-tree sort-merges runs in order to (1) bound the number of runs that a lookup has to access in secondary storage, and to (2) remove obsolete entries to reclaim space. It organizes runs into $L$ conceptual levels of exponentially increasing sizes. Level 0 is the buffer in main memory, and runs belonging to all other levels are in secondary storage.

The balance between the I/O cost of merging and the I/O cost of lookups and space-amplification can be tuned using two knobs. The first knob is the size ratio $T$ between the capacities of adjacent levels; $T$ controls the number of levels of LSM-tree and thus the overall number of times that an entry gets merged across levels. The second knob is the merge policy, which controls the number of times an entry gets merged within a level. All designs today use either one of two merge policies: tiering or leveling (e.g., Cassandra and RocksDB use tiering and leveling by default, respectively [6, 29]). With tiering, we merge runs within a level only when the level reaches capacity [33]. With leveling, we merge runs within a level whenever a new run comes in [41]. We compare these policies in Figure 2 (with a size ratio of 4 and a buffer size of one entry²). In both cases, the merge is triggered by the buffer flushing and causing Level 1 to reach capacity. With tiering, all runs at Level 1 get merged into a new run that gets placed at Level 2. With leveling, the merge also includes the preexisting run at Level 2. We formally study this design space in the next section. We discuss further details of merge mechanics in Appendix D and other log-structured designs in Appendix E.

² In practice, the buffer size is typically set between 2 and 64 MB [29, 32], but we use a size of one entry in this example for ease of illustration.

**Number of Levels.** The buffer at Level 0 has a capacity of $B · P$ entries, where $B$ is the number of entries that fit into a storage block, and $P$ is the size of the buffer in terms of storage blocks. In general, Level $i$ has a capacity of $B · P · T^i$ entries, and the capacity at the largest level can be approximated as having $N · \frac{T-1}{T}$ entries. To derive the number of levels, we divide the capacity at the largest level by the capacity of the buffer and take log base $T$ of the quotient, as shown in Equation 1.

$$L = \left\lceil \log_T\left(\frac{N}{B · P} · \frac{T-1}{T}\right) \right\rceil \tag{1}$$

We restrict the size ratio to the domain of $2 ≤ T ≤ T_{lim}$, where $T_{lim}$ is defined as $\frac{N}{B · P}$. As the size ratio increases and approaches $T_{lim}$, the number of levels decreases and approaches 1. Increasing $T$ beyond $T_{lim}$ has no structural impact. Furthermore, restricting $T$ to be 2 or greater ensures that the resulting run from a merge operation at level $i$ is never large enough to move beyond level $i + 1$. In other words, this ensures that runs do not skip levels. Thus, the highest possible number of levels $L_{max}$ is $\left\lceil \log_2\left(\frac{N}{B · P} · \frac{1}{2}\right) \right\rceil$ (i.e., when the size ratio is set to 2).

**Finding Entries.** Since entries are updated out-of-place, multiple versions of an entry with the same key may exist across multiple levels (and even across runs within a level with tiering). To ensure that a lookup is always able to find the most recent version of an entry, LSM-tree takes the following measures. (1) When an entry is inserted into the buffer and the buffer already contains an entry with the same key, the newer entry replaces the older one. (2) When two runs that contain an entry with the same key are merged, only the entry from the newer run is kept because it is more recent. (3) To be able to infer the order at which different entries with the same key across different runs were inserted, a run can only be merged with the next older or the next younger run. Overall, these rules ensure that if there are two runs that contain different versions of the same entry, the younger run contains the newer version.

**Point Lookups.** A point lookup finds the most recent version of an entry by traversing the levels from smallest to largest, and runs within a level from youngest to oldest with tiering. It terminates when it finds the first entry with a matching key.

**Range Lookups.** A range lookup has to find the most recent versions of all entries within the target key range. It does this by sort-merging the relevant key range across all runs at all levels. While sort-merging, it identifies entries with the same key across different runs and discards older versions.

**Deletes.** Deletes are supported by adding a one-bit flag to every entry. If a lookup finds that the most recent version of an entry has this flag on, it does not return a value to the application. When a deleted entry is merged with the oldest run, it is discarded as it has replaced all entries with the same key that were inserted prior to it.

**Fragmented Merging.** To smooth out performance slumps due to long merge operations at larger levels, mainstream designs partition runs into files (e.g., 2 to 64 MB [29, 32]) called Sorted String Tables (SSTables), and they merge one SSTable at a time with SSTables with an overlapping key range at the next older run. This technique does not affect the worst-case I/O overhead of merging but only how this overhead gets scheduled across time. For readability throughout the paper, we discuss merge operations as having the granularity of runs, though they can also have the granularity of SSTables.

**Space-Amplification.** The factor by which the presence of obsolete entries amplify storage space is known as space-amplification. Space-amplification has traditionally not been a major concern for data structure design due to the affordability of disks. The advent of SSDs, however, makes space-amplification an important cost concern (e.g., Facebook has recently switched from B-trees to leveled LSM-trees due to their superior space-amplification properties [27]). We include space-amplification as a cost metric to give a complete picture of the designs that we introduce and evaluate.

**Fence Pointers.** All major LSM-tree based key-value stores index the first key of every block of every run in main memory. We call these fence pointers (see Figure 2). Formally, the fence pointers take up $O(N/B)$ space in main memory, and they enable a lookup to find the relevant key-range at every run with one I/O.

**Bloom Filters.** To speed up point lookups, which are common in practice [16, 48], each run has a Bloom filter [14] in main memory, as shown in Figure 2. A Bloom filter is a space-efficient probabilistic data structure used to answer set membership queries. It cannot return a false negative, though it returns a false positive with a tunable false positive rate (FPR). The FPR depends on the ratio between the number of bits allocated to the filter and the number of entries in the set according to the following expression³ [50]:

$$FPR = e^{-(bits/entries) · \ln(2)^2} \tag{2}$$

A point lookup probes a Bloom filter before accessing the corresponding run in storage. If the filter returns a true positive, the lookup accesses the run with one I/O (i.e., using the fence pointers), finds the matching entry, and terminates. If the filter returns a negative, the lookup skips the run thereby saving one I/O. Otherwise, we have a false positive, meaning the lookup wastes one I/O by accessing the run, not finding a matching entry, and having to continue searching for the target key in the next run.

³ Equation 2 assumes that the Bloom filter is using the optimal number of hash functions $\frac{bits}{entries} · \ln(2)$ to minimize the false positive rate.

Bloom filter has a useful property that if it is partitioned into smaller equally-sized Bloom filters with an equal division of entries among them, the FPR of each one of the new partitioned Bloom filters is asymptotically the same as the FPR of the original filter (though slightly higher in practice) [50]. For ease of discussion, we refer to Bloom filters as being non-partitioned, though they can also be partitioned (e.g., per every block of every run) as some designs in industry to enable greater flexibility for space management (e.g., Bloom filters for blocks that are not frequently read by point lookups can be offloaded to storage to save memory) [32].

**Applicability Beyond Key-Value Stores.** In accordance with designs in industry, our discussion assumes that a key is stored adjacently to its value within a run [29, 32]. For readability, all figures in this paper depict entries as keys, but they represent key-value pairs. Our work also applies to applications where there are no values (i.e., the LSM-tree is used to answer set-membership queries on keys), where the values are pointers to data objects stored outside of LSM-tree [39], or where LSM-tree is used as a building block for solving a more complex algorithmic problem (e.g., graph analytics [17], flash translation layer design [23], etc.). We restrict the scope of analysis to the basic operations and size of LSM-tree so that it can easily be applied to each of these other cases.
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节介绍基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储所需的背景知识。图 2 展示了其架构，表 1 列出了本文使用的术语。

*图 2：采用 tiering 和 leveling 合并策略的 LSM-tree 概览。*

| 术语 | 定义 | 单位 |
|---|---|---|
| $N$ | 条目总数 | 条目 |
| $L$ | 层数 | 层 |
| $L_{max}$ | 可能的最大层数 | 层 |
| $B$ | 一个存储块能够容纳的条目数 | 条目 |
| $P$ | 以存储块计的缓冲区大小 | 块 |
| $T$ | 相邻层之间的大小比率 | |
| $T_{lim}$ | $L$ 收敛到 1 时的大小比率 | |
| $M$ | 分配给 Bloom 过滤器的主存 | 位 |
| $p_i$ | 第 $i$ 层 Bloom 过滤器的假阳性率 | |
| $s$ | 范围查询的选择度 | 条目 |
| $R$ | 零结果点查询成本 | I/O 次数 |
| $V$ | 非零结果点查询成本 | I/O 次数 |
| $Q$ | 范围查询成本 | I/O 次数 |
| $W$ | 更新成本 | I/O 次数 |
| $K$ | 第 1 层到第 $L - 1$ 层的sorted run数量上界 | sorted run |
| $Z$ | 第 $L$ 层的sorted run数量上界 | sorted run |
| $μ$ | 存储设备顺序访问速度与随机访问速度之比 | |
| $ϕ$ | 存储设备写入速度与读取速度之比 | |

*表 1：本文全文使用的术语表。*

**LSM-Tree 结构。** LSM-tree 针对写密集型工作负载进行优化。对于当今的系统而言，这是一个重要的性能目标，因为应用写入的比例在持续增加（例如，Yahoo! 在 2012 年报告称，其 Web 服务所接收的请求中写入比例为 50%，并预计会继续加速增长 [48]）。为了优化写入，LSM-tree 首先在主存中缓冲所有更新、插入和删除操作（除非另有说明，以下统称为更新），如图 2 所示。当缓冲区填满时，LSM-tree 将缓冲区作为一个sorted run刷写到二级存储。LSM-tree 对sorted run进行排序合并，以便：(1) 限制一次查询必须在二级存储中访问的sorted run数量；(2) 删除过时条目以回收空间。它将sorted run组织成 $L$ 个概念层，各层大小呈指数增长。第 0 层是主存中的缓冲区，其他所有层的sorted run都位于二级存储中。

合并的 I/O 成本与查询和空间放大的 I/O 成本之间的平衡，可以通过两个调节旋钮来调整。第一个旋钮是相邻层容量之间的大小比率 $T$；$T$ 控制 LSM-tree 的层数，进而控制一个条目跨层合并的总次数。第二个旋钮是合并策略，它控制一个条目在一层内部被合并的次数。当今所有设计都采用两种合并策略之一：tiering 或 leveling（例如，Cassandra 和 RocksDB 默认分别采用 tiering 和 leveling [6, 29]）。使用 tiering 时，只有当一层达到容量上限时，才合并该层内的sorted run [33]。使用 leveling 时，每当一个新的sorted run进入某层，就合并该层内的sorted run [41]。图 2 对比了这两种策略（大小比率为 4，缓冲区大小为一个条目²）。在这两种情况下，缓冲区刷写导致第 1 层达到容量上限，从而触发合并。使用 tiering 时，第 1 层的所有sorted run被合并成一个新的sorted run，并放入第 2 层。使用 leveling 时，合并还包括第 2 层中预先存在的sorted run。下一节将对这一设计空间作形式化研究。附录 D 讨论合并机制的更多细节，附录 E 讨论其他日志结构化设计。

² 在实践中，缓冲区大小通常设置为 2 至 64 MB [29, 32]，但为便于说明，本例使用一个条目的大小。

**层数。** 第 0 层缓冲区的容量为 $B · P$ 个条目，其中 $B$ 是一个存储块能够容纳的条目数，$P$ 是以存储块计的缓冲区大小。一般而言，第 $i$ 层的容量为 $B · P · T^i$ 个条目，而最大层的容量可以近似为 $N · \frac{T-1}{T}$ 个条目。为了推导层数，我们用最大层的容量除以缓冲区容量，再对商取以 $T$ 为底的对数，如公式 1 所示。

$$L = \left\lceil \log_T\left(\frac{N}{B · P} · \frac{T-1}{T}\right) \right\rceil \tag{1}$$

我们将大小比率限制在 $2 ≤ T ≤ T_{lim}$ 的范围内，其中 $T_{lim}$ 定义为 $\frac{N}{B · P}$。随着大小比率增大并接近 $T_{lim}$，层数会减少并趋近于 1。继续将 $T$ 增大到 $T_{lim}$ 以上不会产生结构上的影响。此外，将 $T$ 限制为不小于 2，可以确保第 $i$ 层合并操作所产生的sorted run不会大到越过第 $i + 1$ 层。换言之，这能确保sorted run不会跳层。因此，可能的最大层数 $L_{max}$ 为 $\left\lceil \log_2\left(\frac{N}{B · P} · \frac{1}{2}\right) \right\rceil$（即大小比率设置为 2 时）。

**查找条目。** 由于条目采用异地更新，同一键对应的多个条目版本可能存在于多个层中（使用 tiering 时，甚至可能存在于同一层的多个sorted run中）。为了保证查询总能找到条目的最新版本，LSM-tree 采取以下措施。(1) 当一个条目被插入缓冲区，而缓冲区中已经包含具有相同键的条目时，较新的条目会替换较旧的条目。(2) 当包含相同键对应条目的两个sorted run合并时，只保留较新sorted run中的条目，因为它的版本更新。(3) 为了能够推断不同sorted run中具有相同键的不同条目的插入顺序，一个sorted run只能与紧邻的更旧sorted run或紧邻的更新sorted run合并。总体而言，这些规则确保：如果两个sorted run包含同一条目的不同版本，则较新的sorted run包含更新的版本。

**点查询。** 点查询从最小层到最大层遍历各层；使用 tiering 时，还会在每层内从最新sorted run到最旧sorted run进行遍历，以查找条目的最新版本。查询在找到第一个键匹配的条目时终止。

**范围查询。** 范围查询必须找到目标键范围内所有条目的最新版本。它通过对所有层的所有sorted run中的相关键范围进行排序合并来实现这一点。在排序合并过程中，它识别不同sorted run中具有相同键的条目，并丢弃较旧版本。

**删除。** 系统通过为每个条目增加一个一位标志来支持删除。如果查询发现某条目最新版本的这个标志已置位，就不会向应用返回值。当已删除条目与最旧sorted run合并时，它会被丢弃，因为它已经取代了此前插入的所有同键条目。

**碎片化合并。** 为了缓解较大层中的长时间合并操作所导致的性能骤降，主流设计将sorted run划分成称为有序字符串表（Sorted String Table，SSTable）的文件（例如 2 至 64 MB [29, 32]），并且每次将一个 SSTable 与下一个更旧sorted run中键范围重叠的 SSTable 进行合并。这项技术不会影响合并的最坏情况 I/O 开销，只会影响该开销在时间维度上的调度方式。为了提高全文的可读性，我们以sorted run为粒度讨论合并操作，尽管合并也可以采用 SSTable 粒度。

**空间放大。** 过时条目的存在使存储空间扩大的倍数称为空间放大。由于磁盘价格低廉，空间放大传统上并不是数据结构设计的主要关注点。然而，SSD 的出现使空间放大成为一个重要的成本问题（例如，由于 leveled LSM-tree 具有更优的空间放大特性，Facebook 最近已从 B-tree 转向 leveled LSM-tree [27]）。我们将空间放大纳入成本指标，以完整呈现本文所提出并评估的设计。

**围栏指针。** 所有主流的基于 LSM-tree 的键值存储都会在主存中索引每个sorted run的每个块的第一个键。我们将其称为围栏指针（见图 2）。形式化地说，围栏指针在主存中占用 $O(N/B)$ 的空间，并使查询能够以一次 I/O 找到每个sorted run中的相关键范围。

**Bloom 过滤器。** 为了加速实践中常见的点查询 [16, 48]，每个sorted run在主存中都有一个 Bloom 过滤器 [14]，如图 2 所示。Bloom 过滤器是一种用于回答集合成员资格查询的空间高效概率数据结构。它不会返回假阴性，但会以可调的假阳性率（FPR）返回假阳性。FPR 取决于为过滤器分配的位数与集合中条目数之比，满足下列表达式³ [50]：

$$FPR = e^{-(bits/entries) · \ln(2)^2} \tag{2}$$

点查询在访问存储中相应的sorted run之前，会先探查 Bloom 过滤器。如果过滤器返回真阳性，查询便以一次 I/O 访问该sorted run（即使用围栏指针），找到匹配条目并终止。如果过滤器返回阴性，查询会跳过该sorted run，从而节省一次 I/O。否则就出现了假阳性，这意味着查询浪费了一次 I/O：它访问了sorted run，却没有找到匹配条目，因而不得不在下一个sorted run中继续搜索目标键。

³ 公式 2 假设 Bloom 过滤器采用最优哈希函数数量 $\frac{bits}{entries} · \ln(2)$，以使假阳性率最小。

Bloom 过滤器有一个有用的性质：如果将其划分为多个大小相等的较小 Bloom 过滤器，并将条目等量分配给它们，那么每个新划分的 Bloom 过滤器的 FPR 渐近地等于原过滤器的 FPR（尽管在实践中会略高）[50]。为便于讨论，我们将 Bloom 过滤器视为未分区的，不过它们也可以像工业界的一些设计那样进行分区（例如，为每个sorted run的每个块设置一个分区），从而提高空间管理的灵活性（例如，可以将点查询不经常读取的块所对应的 Bloom 过滤器卸载到存储中，以节省内存）[32]。

**键值存储之外的适用性。** 与工业界的设计一致，我们的讨论假设在一个sorted run内，键与其值相邻存储 [29, 32]。为提高可读性，本文所有图都将条目描绘成键，但它们实际代表键值对。我们的工作也适用于以下应用：不存在值的应用（即 LSM-tree 用于回答有关键的集合成员资格查询）；值是指向 LSM-tree 外部所存储数据对象的指针的应用 [39]；或者将 LSM-tree 用作构建模块来解决更复杂算法问题的应用（例如图分析 [17]、闪存转换层设计 [23] 等）。我们将分析范围限制在 LSM-tree 的基本操作和大小上，以便分析能够轻松应用于上述其他情形。

sections.title: 3 DESIGN SPACE AND PROBLEM ANALYSIS
sections.originalMarkdown:
We now analyze how the worse-case space-amplification and I/O costs of updates and lookups derive from across different levels with respect to the merge policy and size ratio. To analyze updates and lookups, we use the disk access model [1] to count the number of I/Os per operation, where an I/O is a block read or written from secondary storage. The results are summarized in Figures 3 and 4.

**Analyzing Updates.** The I/O cost of updating an entry is paid through the subsequent merge operations that the updated entry participates in. Our analysis assumes a worst-case workload whereby all updates target entries at the largest level. This means that an obsolete entry does not get removed until its corresponding updated entry has reached the largest level. As a result, every entry gets merged across all levels (i.e., rather than getting discarded at some smaller level by a more recent entry and thereby reducing overhead for later merge operations).

With tiering, an entry gets merged $O(1)$ time per level across $O(L)$ levels. We divide this by the block size $B$ since every I/O during a merge operation copies $B$ entries from the original runs to the new run. Thus, the amortized I/O cost for one update is $O(L/B)$ I/O.

With leveling, the $j$th run that arrives at Level $i$ triggers a merge operation involving the existing run at Level $i$, which is the merged product of the previous $T-j$ runs that arrived since the last time Level $i$ was empty. Overall, an entry gets merged on average $T/2$, or $O(T)$, times per level before that level reaches capacity, and across $O(L)$ levels for a total of $O(T · L)$ merge operations. As with tiering, we divide this by the block size $B$ to get the amortized I/O cost for one update: $O(L · T/B)$ I/O.

**Figure 3:** In the worst-case, updates and short range lookups derive their I/O cost equally from access to all levels, whereas point lookups and long range lookups derive their I/O cost mostly from access to the largest level.

We now take a closer look at how update cost derives from across different levels. With tiering, Level $i$ fills up every $B · P · T^i$ application updates, and the resulting merge operation copies $B · P · T^i$ entries. With leveling, a merge operation takes place at Level $i$ every $B · P · T^{i-1}$ updates (i.e., every time that a new run comes in), and it copies on average $B · P · T^i/2$ entries. By dividing the number of copied entries by the frequency of a merge operation at Level $i$ for either leveling or tiering, we observe that in the long run the amount of work done by merge operations at every level is the same, as shown with the cost breakdown in Figure 3 (A). The intuition is that while merge operations at larger levels do exponentially more work, they are also exponentially less frequent.

**Analyzing Point Lookups.** To analyze the worst-case point lookup cost, we focus on zero-result point lookups (i.e., to non-existing keys) because they maximize the long-run average of wasted I/Os (i.e., to runs that do not contain a target key). Zero-result point lookups are common in practice (e.g., in insert-if-not-exist operations) [16, 48], and we extend their analysis to point lookups that target existing entries in the next section.

The I/O cost for a zero-result point lookup is highest when every Bloom filter returns a false positive. In this case, a point lookup issues one I/O to every run, amounting to $O(L)$ wasted I/Os with leveling and $O(T · L)$ wasted I/Os with tiering. In practice, however, the Bloom filters eliminate most I/Os to runs that do not contain the target key; key-value stores in industry use 10 bits per entry for every Bloom filters leading to a false positive rate (FPR) of ≈ 1% for each filter [29, 32, 34]. For this reason, we focus on the expected worst-case point lookup cost, which estimates the number of I/Os issued by point lookups as a long-run average with respect to the Bloom filters' FPRs. We estimate this cost as the sum of FPRs across all the Bloom filters. The reason is that the I/O cost of probing an individual run is an independent random variable with an expected value equal to the corresponding Bloom filter's FPR, and the expected sum of multiple independent random variables is equal to the sum of their individual expected values [44].

In key-value stores in industry, the number of bits per entry for Bloom filters across all levels is the same [7, 29, 32, 34, 48]. Therefore, the Bloom filter(s) at the largest level are larger than the filters at all smaller levels combined, as they represent exponentially more entries. Thus, the FPR $p_L$ at the largest level is upper bounded using Equation 2 to $O(e^{-M/N})$, where $M$ is the memory budget for all filters and $N$ is the number of entries in the system. Since all other FPRs are the same as $p_L$, the expected point lookup cost is the product of $p_L$ and the number of runs in the system: $O(e^{-M/N} · L)$ I/Os with leveling and $O(e^{-M/N} · L · T)$ I/Os with tiering.

The most recent paper on this issue named Monkey [22] shows that setting the same number of bits per entry for filters across all levels does not minimize the expected number of wasted I/Os. Instead, Monkey reallocates ≈ 1 bit per entry from the filter(s) at the largest level, and it uses these bits to set the number of bits per entry across smaller levels as an increasing arithmetic progression: Level $i$ gets $a + b · (L-i)$ bits per entry, where $a$ and $b$ are small constants. This causes a small, asymptotically constant increase to the FPR at the largest level and an exponential decrease to the FPRs across smaller levels, as they contain exponentially less entries. Since the FPRs are exponentially decreasing for smaller levels, the sum of FPRs converges to a multiplicative constant that is independent of the number of levels. As a result, Monkey shaves a factor of $L$ from the complexity of point lookups leading to $O(e^{-M/N})$ I/Os with leveling and $O(e^{-M/N} · T)$ I/Os with tiering, as we illustrate in Figure 3 (B). It is always beneficial to use Monkey, for zero and non-zero result point lookups alike and with any kind of skew [22].

Overall, we observe that point lookup cost using Monkey derives mostly from the largest level, as smaller levels have exponentially lower FPRs and so access to them is exponentially less probable. As we will see at the end of this section, this opens up avenues for further optimization.

**Analyzing Space-Amplification.** We define space-amplification as the factor $amp$ by which the overall number of entries $N$ is greater than the number of unique entries $unq$: $amp = N/unq - 1$. To analyze the worst-case space-amplification, we observe that levels 1 to $L-1$ of LSM-tree comprise a fraction of $1/T$ of its capacity whereas level $L$ comprises the remaining fraction of $(T-1)/T$ of its capacity. With leveling, the worst-case space-amplification occurs when entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ are all updates to different entries at Level $L$ thereby rendering at most a fraction of $1/T$ entries at level $L$ obsolete. Space-amplification is therefore $O(1/T)$, as shown in Figure 4 (A). For example, in production environments using SSDs at Facebook, RocksDB uses leveling and a size ratio of 10 to bound space-amplification to ≈ 10% [27]. With tiering, the worst-case occurs when entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ are all updates to different entries at Level $L$, and where every run at Level $L$ contains the same set of entries. In this case, Level $L$ entirely consists of obsolete entries, and so space-amplification is $O(T)$ as level $L$ is larger by a factor of $T-1$ than all other levels combined. Overall, space-amplification with both leveling and tiering in the worst case derives mostly from the presence of obsolete entries at the largest level.

4 In fact, if the largest level is not filled to capacity, space-amplification may be higher than $1/T$, but it is straightforward to dynamically enforce the size ratio across levels to $T$ during runtime by adjusting the capacities at levels 1 to $L-1$ to guarantee the upper bound of $O(1/T)$ as in RocksDB [27].

**Figure 4:** Space-amplification is $O(1/T)$ with leveling and $O(T)$ with tiering.

**Analyzing Range Lookups.** We denote the selectivity of a range lookup $s$ as the number of unique entries across all runs that fall within the target key range. A range lookup scans and sort-merges the target key range across all runs, and it eliminates obsolete entries from the result set. For analysis, we consider a range lookup to be long if the number of blocks accessed is at least twice as large as the maximum possible number of levels: $s/B > 2 · L_{max}$. Under uniformly randomly distributed updates, this condition implies with a high probability that most entries within a target key range are at the largest level. For all practical purposes, we generalize the treatment of long and short range lookups in Section 4.2.

A short range lookup issues approximately one I/O to every run amounting to $O(T · L)$ I/Os with tiering and $O(L)$ I/Os with leveling, as shown in Figure 3 (C). For a long range lookup, the size of the result set before eliminating obsolete entries is on average the product of its selectivity and space-amplification. We divide this product by the block size to get the I/O cost: $O(T · s/B)$ with tiering and $O(s/B)$ with leveling, as shown in Figure 3 (D).

A key distinction is that a short range lookup derives its cost approximately equally from across all levels, whereas a long range lookup derives most of its cost from access to the largest level.

**Mapping the Design Space to the Trade-Off Space.** There is an intrinsic trade-off between update cost on one hand and the costs of lookups and space-amplification on the other. We illustrate this trade-off in conceptual Figure 5, whereon the solid line plots the different costs of lookups and space-amplification on the y-axis against update cost on the x-axis for both leveling and tiering as we vary the size ratio, all based on the properties in Figures 3 and 4. When the size ratio is set to its limiting value of $T_{lim}$ (meaning there is only one level in storage), a tiered LSM-tree degenerates into a log whereas a leveled LSM-tree degenerates into a sorted array. When the size ratio is set to its lower limit of 2, the performance characteristics for leveling and tiering converge as their behaviors become identical: the number of levels is the same and a merge operation is triggered at every level when the second run comes in. In general, as the size ratio increases with leveling/tiering, lookup cost and space-amplification decrease/increase and update cost increases/decreases. Thus, the trade-off space is partitioned: leveling has strictly better lookup costs and space-amplification and strictly worse update cost than tiering.

**Figure 5:** How far down is it possible to push the curve of possible space-time trade-offs?

**The Holy Grail.** The solid line in Figure 5 reflects the properties of Monkey, the current state of the art. Figure 5 also shows a dotted line labeled the elusive optimal. The question guiding our research is whether other designs are possible with space-time trade-offs that more closely approach or even reach the elusive optimal.

**The Opportunity: Removing Superfluous Merging.** We have identified an asymmetry: point lookup cost, long range lookup cost, and space-amplification derive mostly from the largest level, while update cost derives equally from across all levels. This means that merge operations at smaller levels significantly amplify update cost while yielding a comparatively insignificant benefit for space-amplification, point lookups, and long range lookups. There is therefore an opportunity of a merge policy that merges less at smaller levels.
sections.translationMarkdown:
现在，我们分析在不同合并策略和大小比下，更新与查询的最坏情况空间放大和 I/O 成本如何来自不同层。为了分析更新和查询，我们采用磁盘访问模型 [1]，统计每次操作的 I/O 次数，其中一次 I/O 是从二级存储读取或向其写入一个块。结果汇总于图 3 和图 4。

**更新分析。** 更新一个条目的 I/O 成本通过该条目随后参与的合并操作来支付。我们的分析假设一种最坏情况工作负载，其中所有更新都以最大层中的条目为目标。这意味着，过时条目要等到对应的更新条目到达最大层后才会被删除。因此，每个条目都会跨所有层参与合并，而不会在某个较小层被更新的条目丢弃、从而降低后续合并操作的开销。

在 tiering 中，一个条目在每层合并 $O(1)$ 次，共经过 $O(L)$ 层。由于合并操作中的每次 I/O 都会把 $B$ 个条目从原有 run 复制到新 run，因此我们将其除以块大小 $B$。所以，一次更新的摊销 I/O 成本为 $O(L/B)$ 次 I/O。

在 leveling 中，到达第 $i$ 层的第 $j$ 个 run 会触发一次合并，该合并涉及第 $i$ 层已有的 run；这个已有 run 是自第 $i$ 层上次为空以来，先前到达的 $T-j$ 个 run 的合并结果。总体而言，一个条目在该层达到容量上限之前，平均每层被合并 $T/2$ 次，即 $O(T)$ 次；它跨越 $O(L)$ 层，因此总共参与 $O(T · L)$ 次合并操作。与 tiering 一样，我们将其除以块大小 $B$，得到一次更新的摊销 I/O 成本：$O(L · T/B)$ 次 I/O。

**图 3：** 在最坏情况下，更新和短范围查询的 I/O 成本均匀地来自对所有层的访问，而点查询和长范围查询的 I/O 成本主要来自对最大层的访问。

下面进一步观察更新成本如何来自不同层。在 tiering 中，每经过 $B · P · T^i$ 次应用更新，第 $i$ 层就会填满，由此产生的合并操作会复制 $B · P · T^i$ 个条目。在 leveling 中，每经过 $B · P · T^{i-1}$ 次更新，第 $i$ 层就会发生一次合并操作（即每当一个新 run 到达时），并且平均复制 $B · P · T^i/2$ 个条目。对于 leveling 或 tiering，用复制的条目数除以第 $i$ 层合并操作的发生频率，可以看出长期而言，每一层的合并操作所完成的工作量都相同，如图 3(A) 的成本分解所示。直观上，较大层的合并操作虽然要完成指数级更多的工作，但其发生频率也呈指数级降低。

**点查询分析。** 为分析最坏情况下的点查询成本，我们关注零结果点查询，即查询不存在的键，因为这种查询会使浪费的 I/O（即访问不包含目标键的 run）的长期平均值最大。零结果点查询在实践中很常见，例如 insert-if-not-exist 操作 [16, 48]；下一节会把这一分析扩展到以现有条目为目标的点查询。

当每个布隆过滤器都返回假阳性时，零结果点查询的 I/O 成本最高。此时，点查询会对每个 run 发起一次 I/O；leveling 会浪费 $O(L)$ 次 I/O，tiering 会浪费 $O(T · L)$ 次 I/O。然而在实践中，布隆过滤器会消除大部分对不包含目标键的 run 的 I/O；工业界的键值存储为每个布隆过滤器中的每个条目使用 10 bit，使每个过滤器的假阳性率（FPR）约为 1% [29, 32, 34]。因此，我们关注期望最坏情况点查询成本，它依据布隆过滤器的 FPR，估计点查询所发起 I/O 次数的长期平均值。我们将该成本估算为所有布隆过滤器 FPR 之和。原因是，探测单个 run 的 I/O 成本是一个独立随机变量，其期望值等于相应布隆过滤器的 FPR；多个独立随机变量之和的期望，等于各变量期望之和 [44]。

在工业界的键值存储中，各层布隆过滤器的每条目 bit 数相同 [7, 29, 32, 34, 48]。由于最大层表示的条目数呈指数级更多，其布隆过滤器的大小会超过所有较小层过滤器大小之和。因此，根据公式 2，最大层的 FPR $p_L$ 的上界为 $O(e^{-M/N})$，其中 $M$ 是所有过滤器的内存预算，$N$ 是系统中的条目数。由于其他所有 FPR 都与 $p_L$ 相同，期望点查询成本等于 $p_L$ 与系统中 run 数量的乘积：leveling 为 $O(e^{-M/N} · L)$ 次 I/O，tiering 为 $O(e^{-M/N} · L · T)$ 次 I/O。

关于这一问题，最新的论文 Monkey [22] 表明，为各层过滤器设置相同的每条目 bit 数，并不能最小化浪费 I/O 的期望次数。相反，Monkey 从最大层的过滤器中为每个条目重新分配约 1 bit，并用这些 bit 将较小层的每条目 bit 数设置为递增的等差数列：第 $i$ 层的每个条目得到 $a + b · (L-i)$ bit，其中 $a$ 和 $b$ 是较小的常数。由于较小层包含的条目数呈指数级减少，这会使最大层的 FPR 出现一个较小的、渐近为常数的增幅，同时使较小层的 FPR 呈指数级下降。由于较小层的 FPR 呈指数级下降，FPR 之和会收敛到一个与层数无关的乘法常数。因此，Monkey 从点查询的复杂度中消去了因子 $L$，使 leveling 的 I/O 成本变为 $O(e^{-M/N})$，tiering 的 I/O 成本变为 $O(e^{-M/N} · T)$，如图 3(B) 所示。对于零结果和非零结果点查询，以及任何类型的数据偏斜，使用 Monkey 总是有利的 [22]。

总体而言，我们观察到，采用 Monkey 时，点查询成本主要来自最大层；因为较小层的 FPR 呈指数级降低，所以访问这些层的概率也呈指数级降低。正如本节末尾将看到的，这为进一步优化提供了机会。

**空间放大分析。** 我们将空间放大定义为总条目数 $N$ 超过唯一条目数 $unq$ 的倍数 $amp$：$amp = N/unq - 1$。为分析最坏情况下的空间放大，我们注意到，LSM-tree 的第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层占其容量的 $1/T$，而第 $L$ 层占剩余的 $(T-1)/T$。对于 leveling，当第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层的条目全部是对第 $L$ 层不同条目的更新，从而使第 $L$ 层至多 $1/T$ 的条目过时时，会出现最坏情况空间放大。因此，空间放大为 $O(1/T)$，如图 4(A) 所示。例如，在 Facebook 使用 SSD 的生产环境中，RocksDB 采用大小比为 10 的 leveling，将空间放大限制在约 10% [27]。对于 tiering，最坏情况出现在第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层的条目全部是对第 $L$ 层不同条目的更新，并且第 $L$ 层的每个 run 都包含同一组条目时。此时，第 $L$ 层完全由过时条目构成；由于第 $L$ 层比其他所有层的总和大 $T-1$ 倍，空间放大为 $O(T)$。总体而言，在最坏情况下，leveling 和 tiering 的空间放大都主要源于最大层中存在的过时条目。

4 实际上，如果最大层没有填满，空间放大可能高于 $1/T$；不过，可以像 RocksDB [27] 那样，在运行时调整第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层的容量，动态强制各层的大小比为 $T$，从而保证 $O(1/T)$ 的上界。

**图 4：** leveling 的空间放大为 $O(1/T)$，tiering 的空间放大为 $O(T)$。

**范围查询分析。** 我们将范围查询的选择率 $s$ 定义为所有 run 中落入目标键范围的唯一条目数。范围查询会扫描所有 run 中的目标键范围并进行排序合并，同时从结果集中消除过时条目。在分析中，如果访问的块数至少为最大可能层数的两倍，即 $s/B > 2 · L_{max}$，我们就认为该范围查询是长范围查询。在更新均匀随机分布的情况下，这一条件意味着目标键范围内的大多数条目很可能位于最大层。出于所有实际目的，我们在第 4.2 节中推广了长范围查询和短范围查询的处理方式。

短范围查询大约会对每个 run 发起一次 I/O，因此 tiering 需要 $O(T · L)$ 次 I/O，leveling 需要 $O(L)$ 次 I/O，如图 3(C) 所示。对于长范围查询，在消除过时条目之前，结果集的大小平均为其选择率与空间放大的乘积。将这一乘积除以块大小即可得到 I/O 成本：tiering 为 $O(T · s/B)$，leveling 为 $O(s/B)$，如图 3(D) 所示。

关键区别在于，短范围查询的成本大致均匀地来自所有层，而长范围查询的大部分成本来自对最大层的访问。

**将设计空间映射到权衡空间。** 更新成本与查询成本及空间放大之间存在内在权衡。我们在概念图 5 中展示了这一权衡：根据图 3 和图 4 的性质，在改变大小比时，实线分别针对 leveling 和 tiering，在横轴上绘制更新成本，在纵轴上绘制各种查询成本和空间放大。当大小比被设置为极限值 $T_{lim}$ 时，即存储中只有一层，tiered LSM-tree 会退化成日志，而 leveled LSM-tree 会退化成有序数组。当大小比被设置为下限 2 时，leveling 和 tiering 的性能特征会收敛，因为两者的行为变得相同：层数相同，并且每层都会在第二个 run 到达时触发合并操作。一般而言，随着 leveling/tiering 的大小比增大，查询成本和空间放大分别降低/增大，而更新成本分别增大/降低。因此，权衡空间被分割开来：leveling 的查询成本和空间放大严格优于 tiering，但更新成本严格劣于 tiering。

**图 5：** 可能的时空权衡曲线究竟能够向下推进多远？

**终极目标。** 图 5 中的实线反映了当前最先进方案 Monkey 的性质。图 5 还显示了一条标记为“难以企及的最优”的虚线。指导我们研究的问题是：是否存在其他设计，使其时空权衡能够更接近、甚至达到这一难以企及的最优点。

**机会：消除多余合并。** 我们发现了一种不对称性：点查询成本、长范围查询成本和空间放大主要来自最大层，而更新成本则均匀地来自所有层。这意味着，较小层的合并操作会显著放大更新成本，却只能为空间放大、点查询和长范围查询带来相对微不足道的收益。因此，我们有机会设计一种在较小层减少合并的合并策略。

sections.title: 4 LAZY LEVELING, FLUID LSM-TREE, AND DOSTOEVSKY
sections.originalMarkdown: We now present Lazy Leveling, Fluid LSM-Tree, and Dostoevsky to fluidly and dynamically adapt across an expanded LSM-tree design space with richer performance and space trade-offs.
sections.translationMarkdown: 现在，我们介绍 Lazy Leveling、Fluid LSM-Tree 和 Dostoevsky，使系统能够在扩展后的 LSM-tree 设计空间中流畅、动态地调整，并获得更丰富的性能与空间权衡。

sections.title: 4.1 Lazy Leveling
sections.originalMarkdown:
Lazy Leveling is a merge policy that eliminates merging at all but the largest level of LSM-tree. The motivation is that merging at these smaller levels significantly increases update cost while yielding a comparatively insignificant improvement for point lookups, long range lookups, and space-amplification. Relative to leveling, we show that Lazy Leveling (1) improves the cost complexity of updates, (2) maintains the same complexity for point lookups, long range lookups, and space-amplification, and (3) provides competitive performance for short range lookups. We summarize the structure and performance characteristics of Lazy Leveling in Figure 6, and we discuss this figure in detail in the rest of the section.

**Basic Structure.** The top part of Figure 6 illustrates the structure of Lazy Leveling and compares it to tiering and leveling. Lazy leveling at its core is a hybrid of leveling and tiering: it applies leveling at the largest level and tiering at all other levels. As a result, the number of runs at the largest level is 1 and the number of runs at all other levels is at most $T-1$ (i.e., a merge operation takes place when the $T$th run arrives).

**Bloom Filters Allocation.** Next, we show how to keep the cost complexity of point lookups fixed despite having more runs to probe at smaller levels. We do this by optimizing the memory budget among the Bloom filters across different levels. We start by modeling point lookup cost and the filters' overall main memory footprint with respect to the FPRs.

**Figure 6:** A cost breakdown of updates, lookups, and space-amplification with different merge policies. Lazy Leveling achieves the same space-amplification, point lookup cost, and long range lookup cost as leveling while improving update cost and providing a slightly worse yet competitive short range lookup cost.

The worst-case expected number of wasted I/Os per lookup is issued by a zero-result point lookup and is equal to the sum of false positive rates across every run's Bloom filters. We model this cost for Lazy Leveling in Equation 3. The additive term $p_L$ corresponds to the FPR for the single run at Level $L$, and the other term sums up the products of FPRs and number of runs at Levels 1 to $L-1$.

$$R = p_L + (T-1) · ∑_{i=1}^{L-1} p_i \quad \text{where } 0 < p_i < 1 \tag{3}$$

Next, we model the memory footprint $M_i$ for the Bloom filters at Level $i$ with respect to the number of entries $N_i$ and the FPR $p_i$ at that level. We do this by rearranging Equation 2 in terms of bits and applying it to each level. Since the filters at any given level all have the same FPR, we can directly apply this equation regardless of the numbers of runs at a level. The result is $M_i = -N_i · \ln(p_i)/\ln(2)^2$. Next, we express $N_i$ more generally as the product of the capacity at the largest level $N · (T-1)/T$ and a discounting factor to adjust for the capacity at Level $i$: $1/T^{L-i}$. We then sum up the memory footprint across all levels to get the overall memory footprint $M$. The result is Equation 4.

$$M = -\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2} · \frac{T-1}{T} · ∑_{i=1}^{L} \frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}} \tag{4}$$

We now optimize Equations 3 and 4 with respect to each other to find the FPRs that minimize point lookup cost $R$ with respect to a given memory budget $M$. To do so, we use the method of Lagrange multipliers. The full derivation is in Appendix A. The result is Equation 5.

$$p_i = \begin{cases} R · \frac{T-1}{T}, & \text{for } i=L \\ R · \frac{1}{T^{L-i+1}}, & \text{for } 1 ≤ i < L \end{cases} \tag{5}$$

**Zero-Result Point Lookups.** Next, we analyze the cost of zero-result point lookups $R$ with Lazy Leveling. We plug the optimal FPRs from Equation 5 into Equation 4, simplify into closed-form, and rearrange in terms of $R$. The complete derivation is in Appendix B. The result is Equation 6.

$$R = e^{-\frac{M}{N} · \ln(2)^2} · \frac{T^{\frac{T}{T-1}}}{(T-1)T^{\frac{1}{T-1}}} \tag{6}$$

This equation allows to quickly find the optimal FPRs with respect to a given memory budget $M$ by plugging in the corresponding value of $R$ from Equation 6 into Equation 5.

To analyze the complexity of zero-result point lookups, we observe that the multiplicative term at the right-hand side of Equation 6 is a small constant for any value of $T$. Therefore, the cost complexity is $O(e^{-M/N})$, the same as with leveling despite having eliminated most merge operations.

**Memory Requirement.** As the number of entries $N$ grows relative to the memory budget $M$, the FPRs increase and eventually converge to one (starting from larger to smaller levels because the FPR at larger levels is higher). We identify the ratio of bits per entry $M/N$ at which point the FPR at Level $L$ converges to one by plugging in one for $p_L$ in Equation 5, plugging the corresponding value of $R$ into Equation 6, and rearranging in terms of $M/N$.

$$\text{threshold for } \frac{M}{N} = \frac{1}{\ln(2)^2} · \left(\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1} + \frac{\ln(T-1)}{T}\right) \tag{7}$$

Equation 7 has global maximum of $M/N = 1.62$ bits per entry (which occurs when $T$ is set to 3). For mainstream key-value stores used for server applications, the default ratio is an order of magnitude larger, typically 10 [29, 32, 48] or 16 [52], and so the FPRs are all lower than one. For systems with less than 1.62 bits per entry (e.g., mobile devices or sensors), we adapt Lazy Leveling and its analysis in Appendix C by merging more at larger levels.

**Point Lookups for Existing Entries.** The worst-case point lookup cost to an existing entry occurs when the target key is at Level $L$. The expected I/O cost comprises one I/O to Level $L$ plus the sum of FPRs across all other levels (i.e., $R-p_L$) and is given in Equation 8. The cost complexity is $O(1)$ I/Os because the term $R-p_L$ is always less than 1 as long as the memory requirement in Equation 7 holds.

$$V = 1 + R - p_L \tag{8}$$

**Range Lookups.** A short range lookup issues at most $O(T)$ I/Os to each of the first $L-1$ levels and one I/O to the largest level, and so the cost complexity is $O(1 + (L-1) · T)$ I/Os. Note that this expression initially increases as $T$ increases, but as $T$ approaches its limiting value of $T_{lim}$ this term converges to 1 as the additive term $(L-1) · T$ on the right-hand size becomes zero (i.e., at this point Lazy Leveling degenerates into a sorted array).

A long range lookup is dominated by sequential access to Level $L$ because it contains exponentially more entries than all other levels. The cost is $O(s/B)$ I/Os, where $s$ is the size of the target key range relative to the size of the existing key space. This is the same as with leveling despite having eliminated most merge operations.

**Updates.** An updated entry with Lazy Leveling participates in $O(1)$ merge operations per level across Levels 1 to $L-1$ and in $O(T)$ merge operations at Level $L$. The overall number of merge operations per entry is therefore $O(L+T)$, and we divide it by the block size $B$ to get the cost for a single update: $O((L+T)/B)$. This is an improvement over the worst-case cost with leveling.

**Space-Amplification.** In the worst case, every entry at Levels 1 to $L-1$ is an update to an existing entry at Level $L$. Since the fraction of entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ is $1/T$ of the overall number of entries, space-amplification is at most $O(1/T)$. This is the same bound as with leveling despite having eliminated most merge operations.

**Limits.** Figure 7 compares the behaviors of the different merge policies as we vary the size ratio $T$ for each policy from 2 to its limit of $T_{lim}$ (i.e., at which point the number of levels drops to one). Firstly, we observe that these policies converge in terms of performance characteristics when the size ratio $T$ is set to 2 because at this point their behaviors become identical: the number of levels is the same and a merge operation occurs at every level when the second run arrives. Secondly, Part (A) of Figure 7 shows that the improvement that Lazy Leveling achieves for update cost relative to leveling can be traded for point lookup cost by increasing the size ratio. This generates a new trade-off curve between update cost and point lookup cost that dominates leveling, and converges with it again as $T$ approaches $T_{lim}$ (i.e., at which point both merge policies degenerate into a sorted array). Parts (B) shows that the cost of small range lookups is competitive, and part (C) shows that this cost difference becomes negligible as the target range grows.

**Figure 7:** Lazy Leveling offers better trade-offs between updates and point lookups (Part A), worse trade-offs between updates and short range lookups (Part B), and the same trade-offs between updates and long range lookups (Part C).

**Lesson: No Single Merge Policy Rules.** Our analysis in figure Figure 7 shows that no single design dominates the others universally. Lazy leveling is best for combined workloads consisting of updates, point lookups and long range lookups, whereas tiering and leveling are best for workloads comprising mostly updates or mostly lookups, respectively. In the rest of the paper, we take steps towards a unified system that adapts across these designs depending on the application scenario.
sections.translationMarkdown:
Lazy Leveling 是一种除 LSM-tree 最大层之外，消除其他所有层合并操作的合并策略。其动机是：较小层的合并会显著增加更新成本，却只能为点查询、长范围查询和空间放大带来相对微不足道的改善。与 leveling 相比，我们证明 Lazy Leveling：(1) 改善了更新的成本复杂度；(2) 对点查询、长范围查询和空间放大保持相同的复杂度；(3) 为短范围查询提供有竞争力的性能。图 6 汇总了 Lazy Leveling 的结构和性能特征，本节其余部分将详细讨论该图。

**基本结构。** 图 6 上半部分展示了 Lazy Leveling 的结构，并将其与 tiering 和 leveling 比较。Lazy Leveling 的核心是 leveling 与 tiering 的混合体：它在最大层采用 leveling，在其他所有层采用 tiering。因此，最大层的 run 数量为 1，其他每一层的 run 数量至多为 $T-1$，即第 $T$ 个 run 到达时发生合并操作。

**布隆过滤器分配。** 接下来，我们说明即使较小层中需要探测的 run 数量更多，也能如何保持点查询成本复杂度不变。为此，我们在不同层的布隆过滤器之间优化内存预算的分配。我们首先根据 FPR，对点查询成本和过滤器的总体内存占用进行建模。

**图 6：** 不同合并策略下更新、查询和空间放大的成本分解。Lazy Leveling 在改善更新成本的同时，取得了与 leveling 相同的空间放大、点查询成本和长范围查询成本，并且其短范围查询成本虽然稍差，但仍具有竞争力。

每次查询浪费 I/O 次数的最坏情况期望由零结果点查询产生，它等于每个 run 的布隆过滤器假阳性率之和。我们在公式 3 中对 Lazy Leveling 的这一成本进行建模。加法项 $p_L$ 对应第 $L$ 层唯一一个 run 的 FPR，另一项则对第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层的 FPR 与 run 数量的乘积求和。

$$R = p_L + (T-1) · ∑_{i=1}^{L-1} p_i \quad \text{其中 } 0 < p_i < 1 \tag{3}$$

接下来，我们根据第 $i$ 层的条目数 $N_i$ 和 FPR $p_i$，对该层布隆过滤器的内存占用 $M_i$ 建模。具体做法是将公式 2 按 bit 数改写，并将其应用于每一层。由于任意给定层的所有过滤器都具有相同 FPR，因此无论一层中有多少个 run，都可以直接应用该公式。结果为 $M_i = -N_i · \ln(p_i)/\ln(2)^2$。随后，我们把 $N_i$ 更一般地表示为最大层容量 $N · (T-1)/T$ 与一个折减因子的乘积，该因子用于根据第 $i$ 层的容量进行调整：$1/T^{L-i}$。然后，对所有层的内存占用求和，得到总内存占用 $M$。结果如公式 4 所示。

$$M = -\frac{N}{\ln(2)^2} · \frac{T-1}{T} · ∑_{i=1}^{L} \frac{\ln(p_i)}{T^{L-i}} \tag{4}$$

现在，我们对公式 3 和公式 4 进行联合优化，以求出在给定内存预算 $M$ 下，使点查询成本 $R$ 最小的各个 FPR。为此，我们采用拉格朗日乘数法。完整推导见附录 A，结果如公式 5 所示。

$$p_i = \begin{cases} R · \frac{T-1}{T}, & \text{当 } i=L \\ R · \frac{1}{T^{L-i+1}}, & \text{当 } 1 ≤ i < L \end{cases} \tag{5}$$

**零结果点查询。** 接下来，我们分析 Lazy Leveling 的零结果点查询成本 $R$。我们将公式 5 中的最优 FPR 代入公式 4，将其化简为闭式表达式，并按 $R$ 重新整理。完整推导见附录 B，结果如公式 6 所示。

$$R = e^{-\frac{M}{N} · \ln(2)^2} · \frac{T^{\frac{T}{T-1}}}{(T-1)T^{\frac{1}{T-1}}} \tag{6}$$

通过把公式 6 中相应的 $R$ 值代入公式 5，该公式可以针对给定内存预算 $M$ 快速求出最优 FPR。

为分析零结果点查询的复杂度，我们注意到，对于任意 $T$ 值，公式 6 右侧的乘法项都是一个较小的常数。因此，其成本复杂度为 $O(e^{-M/N})$；尽管消除了大多数合并操作，它仍与 leveling 相同。

**内存要求。** 随着条目数 $N$ 相对于内存预算 $M$ 增长，FPR 会增大并最终收敛到 1；由于较大层的 FPR 更高，这一过程从较大层开始，逐渐扩展到较小层。为确定第 $L$ 层 FPR 收敛到 1 时的每条目 bit 数之比 $M/N$，我们在公式 5 中令 $p_L$ 为 1，将对应的 $R$ 值代入公式 6，再按 $M/N$ 重新整理。

$$\frac{M}{N}\text{ 的阈值} = \frac{1}{\ln(2)^2} · \left(\frac{\ln(T)}{T-1} + \frac{\ln(T-1)}{T}\right) \tag{7}$$

公式 7 的全局最大值为每条目 $M/N = 1.62$ bit，此时 $T$ 被设置为 3。对于服务器应用所使用的主流键值存储，默认比值要高一个数量级，通常为 10 [29, 32, 48] 或 16 [52]，所以所有 FPR 都小于 1。对于每条目不足 1.62 bit 的系统，例如移动设备或传感器，我们在附录 C 中通过在较大层执行更多合并，对 Lazy Leveling 及其分析进行了调整。

**现有条目的点查询。** 当目标键位于第 $L$ 层时，对现有条目执行点查询的成本达到最坏情况。期望 I/O 成本由对第 $L$ 层的一次 I/O，加上其他所有层的 FPR 之和，即 $R-p_L$ 构成，如公式 8 所示。只要满足公式 7 的内存要求，$R-p_L$ 项始终小于 1，因此成本复杂度为 $O(1)$ 次 I/O。

$$V = 1 + R - p_L \tag{8}$$

**范围查询。** 短范围查询最多对前 $L-1$ 层中的每一层发起 $O(T)$ 次 I/O，并对最大层发起一次 I/O，因此成本复杂度为 $O(1 + (L-1) · T)$ 次 I/O。注意，随着 $T$ 增大，该表达式最初会增大；但当 $T$ 接近其极限值 $T_{lim}$ 时，这一项会收敛到 1，因为右侧的加法项 $(L-1) · T$ 变为零，即此时 Lazy Leveling 退化成有序数组。

长范围查询由对第 $L$ 层的顺序访问主导，因为该层包含的条目比其他所有层都呈指数级更多。成本为 $O(s/B)$ 次 I/O，其中 $s$ 是目标键范围的大小相对于现有键空间大小的比值。尽管消除了大多数合并操作，这仍与 leveling 相同。

**更新。** 在 Lazy Leveling 中，一个被更新的条目在第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层中，每层参与 $O(1)$ 次合并操作，在第 $L$ 层参与 $O(T)$ 次合并操作。因此，每个条目参与合并操作的总次数为 $O(L+T)$；将其除以块大小 $B$，可得到单次更新的成本：$O((L+T)/B)$。这优于 leveling 的最坏情况成本。

**空间放大。** 在最坏情况下，第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层的每个条目都是对第 $L$ 层中现有条目的更新。由于第 1 层到第 $L-1$ 层的条目占总条目数的 $1/T$，空间放大至多为 $O(1/T)$。尽管消除了大多数合并操作，这一上界仍与 leveling 相同。

**极限。** 图 7 比较了不同合并策略的行为，其中每种策略的大小比 $T$ 都从 2 变化到极限值 $T_{lim}$，此时层数降为 1。首先可以看到，当大小比 $T$ 被设置为 2 时，这些策略的性能特征会收敛，因为此时它们的行为变得相同：层数相同，并且每一层都会在第二个 run 到达时执行合并操作。其次，图 7(A) 表明，通过增大大小比，可以用 Lazy Leveling 相对于 leveling 获得的更新成本改善来换取点查询成本。这会生成一条新的更新成本与点查询成本权衡曲线，它支配 leveling，并在 $T$ 接近 $T_{lim}$ 时再次与 leveling 收敛，即此时两种合并策略都退化成有序数组。图 7(B) 表明，小范围查询的成本具有竞争力；图 7(C) 表明，随着目标范围增大，这一成本差异变得可以忽略。

**图 7：** Lazy Leveling 在更新与点查询之间提供更好的权衡（A 部分），在更新与短范围查询之间提供较差的权衡（B 部分），而在更新与长范围查询之间提供相同的权衡（C 部分）。

**经验：不存在一种合并策略能够统治所有场景。** 我们在图 7 中的分析表明，没有一种设计能在所有场景中普遍支配其他设计。对于由更新、点查询和长范围查询组成的混合工作负载，Lazy Leveling 最优；对于主要由更新或主要由查询组成的工作负载，tiering 和 leveling 分别最优。在本文其余部分，我们将逐步构建一个统一系统，使其能够根据应用场景在这些设计之间自适应。

sections.title: 4.2 Fluid LSM-Tree
sections.originalMarkdown:
To be able to strike all possible trade-offs for different workloads, we next introduce Fluid LSM-tree, a generalization of LSM-tree that enables switching and combining merge policies. It does this by controlling the frequency of merge operations separately for the largest level and for all other levels.

**Basic Structure.** Figure 8 illustrates the basic structure of Fluid LSM-tree. There are at most $Z$ runs at the largest level and at most $K$ runs at each of the smaller levels. To maintain these bounds, every Level $i$ has an active run into which we merge incoming runs from Level $i-1$. Each active run has a size threshold with respect to the capacity of its level: $T/K$ percent for Levels 1 to $L-1$ and $T/Z$ percent for Level $L$. When an active run reaches this threshold, we start a new active run at that level. Ultimately when a level is at capacity, all runs in it get merged and flushed down to the next level.

**Parameterization.** The bounds $K$ and $Z$ are used as tuning parameters that enable Fluid LSM-tree to assume the behaviors of different merge policies.

- $K=1$ and $Z=1$ give leveling.
- $K=T-1$ and $Z=T-1$ give tiering.
- $K=T-1$ and $Z=1$ give Lazy Leveling.

Fluid LSM-tree can transition from Lazy Leveling to tiering by merging less frequently at the largest level by increasing $Z$, or it can transition to leveling by merging more frequently at all other levels by decreasing $K$. Fluid LSM-tree spans all possible trade-offs along and between the curves in Figure 7.

**Bloom Filters Allocation.** Next, we derive the optimal FPRs that minimize point lookup cost with respect to the parameters $K$ and $Z$. The derivation is in Appendix A and the result is Equation 9.

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{R}{Z}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}, & \text{for }i=L,\\
\dfrac{R}{K}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T^{L-i}}, & \text{for }1\le i<L.
\end{cases}
\tag{9}
$$

Equation 9 generalizes the optimal Bloom filters allocation strategy in Monkey [22] across a significantly wider design space, which now, in addition to tiering and leveling, also includes Lazy Leveling as well as custom merge policies with any parameter values for $K$ and $Z$. Next, we model and map the new space-time trade-offs that this expanded design space offers.

**Zero-Result Point Lookups.** We model the cost of zero-result point lookups by plugging the generalized optimal FPRs in Equation 9 into Equation 4, simplifying into closed-form, and rearranging in terms of $R$. The derivation is in Appendix B, experimental validation is in Appendix I, and the result is Equation 10. The generalized complexity is $O(Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$ I/Os.

$$
R=e^{-\frac{M}{N}\cdot\ln(2)^2}\cdot Z^{\frac{T-1}{T}}\cdot K^{\frac{1}{T}}\cdot\frac{T^{\frac{T}{T-1}}}{T-1}
\tag{10}
$$

**Point Lookups for Existing Entries.** The worst-case lookup cost to an existing key occurs when the target key is at the oldest run at the largest level. The expected I/O cost is one I/O to this target run plus the sum of FPRs across all other runs. We use Equation 8 to model this, and we plug in Equation 10 for $R$ and Equation 9 for $p_L$. The generalized complexity is $O(1+Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$.

**Memory Requirement.** In Appendix C, we derive the memory requirement $M/N$ that guarantees that FPRs across all Levels are lower than one. The generalized result is 1.62 bits per entry as in the last subsection, which is well below the default ratio in mainstream systems. In Appendix C, we show how to adapt Fluid LSM-tree to extremely low-memory environments.

**Range Lookups.** A short range lookup issues at most $K$ I/Os per level to the smaller $L-1$ Levels and at most $Z$ I/Os to the largest level for a total of $Z+K\cdot(L-1)$ random I/Os and a cost complexity of $O(Z+K\cdot(L-1))$. A long range lookup continues with a sequential scan to the relevant key range at each run issuing at least $s/B$ sequential I/Os, where $s$ is the number of unique entries in the target key range. To account for obsolete entries, the number of sequential I/Os is amplified by a factor of $1+1/T$ for updated entries at Levels 1 to $L-1$ and $Z$ for updated entries at Level $L$, which we model together as $Z+1/T$. The sequential scan cost is therefore at most $s/B\cdot(Z+1/T)$ I/Os with a complexity of $O(s\cdot Z/B)$ I/Os. The generalized range lookup cost is given in Equation 11 as the sum of costs for short and long range lookups weighted by the constant $\mu$, the amount of which sequential access is faster than random access on a given storage devices (e.g., disk).

$$
Q=K\cdot(L-1)+Z+\frac{1}{\mu}\cdot\frac{s}{B}\cdot\left(Z+\frac{1}{T}\right)
\tag{11}
$$

**Updates.** In the worst case, an entry participates in $O(T/K)$ merge operations within an active run across each of Levels 1 to $L-1$, and in $O(T/Z)$ merge operations within the active run at Level $L$. The overall update cost is the sum of these terms across all levels divided by the block size: $O\left(\frac{T}{B}\cdot\left(\frac{L}{K}+\frac{1}{Z}\right)\right)$. We model this cost more precisely using arithmetic series to obtain Equation 12, which we validate in Appendix I. We divide by the constant $\mu$ since the cost of updates is incurred through sequential merge operations, and we introduce an additional constant $\phi$ to account for the property of some storage devices that writes are more expensive than reads (e.g., flash).

$$
W=\frac{\phi}{\mu\cdot B}\cdot\left(\frac{T-1}{K+1}\cdot(L-1)+\frac{T-1}{Z+1}\right)
\tag{12}
$$

**Space-Amplification.** Levels 1 to $L-1$ contain a fraction of $1/T$ of the dataset, and so they may render up to this fraction of entries obsolete at the largest level. In Level $L$, at most $Z-1$ of the runs may be completely filled with obsolete entries. We model space-amplification as the sum of these terms in Equation 13.

$$
amp=Z-1+\frac{1}{T}
\tag{13}
$$

**Figure 8:** A cost breakdown of updates, lookups, and space-amplification with Fluid LSM-tree.

**Mapping the Design Space.** Figure 9 is an instance of conceptual Figure 7 that uses our cost models to map the different trade-offs with Fluid LSM-tree. We generate Part (A) of Figure 9 by plotting point lookup cost $R$ in Equation 10 against update cost $W$ in Equation 12. We generate Parts (B) and (C) for short and long range lookups by plotting $Q$ in Equation 11 against update cost $W$ in Equation 12 for selectivities $s$ of $10^{-7}$ and $10^{-6}$, respectively. We leave an evaluation of space-amplification for the experimental analysis. The configuration is fixed to a 1TB dataset with 128 byte entries, 4KB storage blocks, and overall 10 bits per entry across the filters[^5]. We generate the curves for leveling, tiering, and Lazy Leveling by using their corresponding fixed values for the parameters $K$ and $Z$, and varying the size ratio $T$. The circle indicates the convergence point of all three merge policies where the size ratio $T$ is set to two. The squares indicate a size ratio of ten, which most mainstream key-value stores use by default in practice [29, 32], to enable comparison of corresponding points across the three sub-figures. The figure also illustrates two transition curves labeled Trans1 and Trans2, which demonstrate how Fluid LSM-tree transitions fluidly across designs thereby achieving trade-offs that would not have been possible using a fixed merge policy.

**Figure 9:** Fluidly shifting between Lazy Leveling, leveling, and tiering.

**Transition 1: Lazy Leveling to Tiering.** We observe in Part (A) of Figure 9 that the curve for Lazy Leveling has an inflection point beyond which decreasing the size ratio degrades update cost. The reason is that update cost is $O((L+T)/B)$, and as we decrease $T$ the value of $L$ grows and comes to dominate $T$. In this example, the inflection point occurs when the size ratio $T$ is set to 5. We generate the curve labeled Transition 1 (Trans1) by fixing $T$ to the inflection point value and instead varying $Z$ from 1 to $T-1$ (4 in this example). The resulting curve dominates both Lazy Leveling and tiering for this part of the design space until it converges with tiering. Thus, Transition 1 enables optimal trade-offs between point lookup cost and update cost as we transition between Lazy Leveling and tiering to optimize more for point lookups or updates, respectively.

**Transition 2: Lazy Leveling to Leveling.** In order to achieve more competitive range lookup costs with Lazy Leveling, we introduce Transition 2. The idea is to vary $K$, the bound on runs at Levels 1 to $L-1$, between 1 and $T-1$ to fluidly transition between Lazy Leveling and leveling. In Figure 9 we generate the curve labeled Trans2 by fixing $K$ to 4 and varying $T$. Part (A) shows that this enables navigating a trade-off curve similar to Lazy Leveling, and parts (B) and (C) show that Trans2 achieves nearly the same range lookup cost as with leveling. Thus, Transition 2 enables fine control over how much we optimize for short range lookups.

**Problem: Finding the Best Tuning.** The space of optimal space-time trade-offs is delineated by leveling, Lazy Leveling, and tiering, and we can reach many other trade-offs in-between by co-tuning the parameters $K$, $Z$ and $T$. The goal is to co-tune these parameters to strike the best trade-off for a particular application. The challenge is finding the optimal configuration as there are many different combinations for these parameters[^6].

[^5]: We set $N=2^{33}$, $E=2^7$, $B=2^5$, and $M=10\cdot2^{33}$.

[^6]: The number of combinations is $O((N/(B\cdot P))^3)$. The reason is that the maximum value of $T$ is $T_{lim}=N/(B\cdot P)$, and $K$ and $Z$ can both be tuned to anything between 1 and $T-1$.
sections.translationMarkdown:
为了能够针对不同工作负载取得所有可能的权衡，我们接下来提出Fluid LSM-tree（Fluid LSM-tree）。它是 LSM 树的一种泛化，支持切换和组合合并策略。其实现方式是分别控制最大层与其他所有层的合并操作频率。

**基本结构。** 图 8 展示了Fluid LSM-tree的基本结构。最大层至多有 $Z$ 个 run，每个较小层至多有 $K$ 个 run。为了维持这些上界，每个第 $i$ 层都有一个活跃 run，从第 $i-1$ 层到达的 run 会被合并到其中。每个活跃 run 都有一个相对于所在层容量的大小阈值：对于第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层，该阈值为 $T/K$；对于第 $L$ 层，该阈值为 $T/Z$。当一个活跃 run 达到该阈值时，我们会在该层启动一个新的活跃 run。最终，当某一层达到容量上限时，其中的所有 run 会被合并并下刷到下一层。

**参数化。** 上界 $K$ 和 $Z$ 被用作调优参数，使Fluid LSM-tree能够呈现不同合并策略的行为。

- $K=1$ 且 $Z=1$ 对应 leveling。
- $K=T-1$ 且 $Z=T-1$ 对应 tiering。
- $K=T-1$ 且 $Z=1$ 对应 Lazy Leveling。

通过增大 $Z$、降低最大层的合并频率，Fluid LSM-tree可以从 Lazy Leveling 过渡到 tiering；通过减小 $K$、提高其他所有层的合并频率，它也可以过渡到 leveling。Fluid LSM-tree覆盖了图 7 中各条曲线之上及曲线之间的所有可能权衡。

**布隆过滤器分配。** 接下来，我们推导关于参数 $K$ 和 $Z$、能够使点查询成本最小的最优 FPR。推导见附录 A，结果为公式 9。

$$
p_i=
\begin{cases}
\dfrac{R}{Z}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}, & \text{当 }i=L,\\
\dfrac{R}{K}\cdot\dfrac{T-1}{T}\cdot\dfrac{1}{T^{L-i}}, & \text{当 }1\le i<L。
\end{cases}
\tag{9}
$$

公式 9 将 Monkey [22] 中的最优布隆过滤器分配策略推广到了一个显著更广的设计空间。除 tiering 和 leveling 之外，该空间现在还包括 Lazy Leveling，以及 $K$ 和 $Z$ 可取任意参数值的定制合并策略。下面，我们对这一扩展设计空间所提供的新时空权衡进行建模和映射。

**零结果点查询。** 我们将公式 9 中广义的最优 FPR 代入公式 4，化简为闭式形式并改写为关于 $R$ 的表达式，从而对零结果点查询的成本进行建模。推导见附录 B，实验验证见附录 I，结果为公式 10。其广义复杂度为 $O(Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$ 次 I/O。

$$
R=e^{-\frac{M}{N}\cdot\ln(2)^2}\cdot Z^{\frac{T-1}{T}}\cdot K^{\frac{1}{T}}\cdot\frac{T^{\frac{T}{T-1}}}{T-1}
\tag{10}
$$

**对现有条目的点查询。** 查询现有键时，最坏情况发生在目标键位于最大层最老的 run 中。期望 I/O 成本等于访问该目标 run 的一次 I/O，加上访问其他所有 run 所对应 FPR 的总和。我们使用公式 8 对其建模，并将公式 10 的 $R$ 和公式 9 的 $p_L$ 代入。其广义复杂度为 $O(1+Z\cdot e^{-M/N})$。

**内存需求。** 在附录 C 中，我们推导了保证所有层的 FPR 均小于 1 所需的内存比例 $M/N$。与上一小节相同，广义结果为每个条目 1.62 bit，远低于主流系统的默认比例。附录 C 还说明了如何使Fluid LSM-tree适应内存极少的环境。

**范围查询。** 一次短范围查询对较小的 $L-1$ 层中每一层至多发出 $K$ 次 I/O，对最大层至多发出 $Z$ 次 I/O，因此随机 I/O 总数为 $Z+K\cdot(L-1)$，成本复杂度为 $O(Z+K\cdot(L-1))$。长范围查询还会在每个 run 中继续顺序扫描相关键范围，至少发出 $s/B$ 次顺序 I/O，其中 $s$ 是目标键范围内不同条目的数量。考虑到过时条目，对于第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层中的更新条目，顺序 I/O 数量会被放大 $1+1/T$ 倍；对于第 $L$ 层中的更新条目，则会被放大 $Z$ 倍。我们将二者统一建模为 $Z+1/T$。因此，顺序扫描成本至多为 $s/B\cdot(Z+1/T)$ 次 I/O，复杂度为 $O(s\cdot Z/B)$ 次 I/O。广义范围查询成本由公式 11 给出，它是短范围查询和长范围查询成本之和，并使用常数 $\mu$ 加权；$\mu$ 表示在给定存储设备（例如磁盘）上顺序访问相对于随机访问快多少。

$$
Q=K\cdot(L-1)+Z+\frac{1}{\mu}\cdot\frac{s}{B}\cdot\left(Z+\frac{1}{T}\right)
\tag{11}
$$

**更新。** 在最坏情况下，一个条目在第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的每一层中，会在活跃 run 内参与 $O(T/K)$ 次合并；在第 $L$ 层的活跃 run 内参与 $O(T/Z)$ 次合并。总更新成本等于这些项在所有层上的总和除以块大小，即 $O\left(\frac{T}{B}\cdot\left(\frac{L}{K}+\frac{1}{Z}\right)\right)$。我们利用等差数列对该成本进行更精确的建模，得到公式 12，并在附录 I 中对其进行验证。由于更新成本是通过顺序合并操作产生的，我们将其除以常数 $\mu$；此外又引入常数 $\phi$，以表示某些存储设备（例如闪存）的写入成本高于读取成本这一特性。

$$
W=\frac{\phi}{\mu\cdot B}\cdot\left(\frac{T-1}{K+1}\cdot(L-1)+\frac{T-1}{Z+1}\right)
\tag{12}
$$

**空间放大。** 第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层包含数据集的 $1/T$，因此它们最多可能使最大层中同样比例的条目变为过时条目。在第 $L$ 层中，至多有 $Z-1$ 个 run 可能完全由过时条目填满。公式 13 将这些项相加，对空间放大进行建模。

$$
amp=Z-1+\frac{1}{T}
\tag{13}
$$

**图 8：** 使用Fluid LSM-tree时，更新、查询和空间放大的成本分解。

**映射设计空间。** 图 9 是概念图 7 的一个具体实例，它使用我们的成本模型映射Fluid LSM-tree的不同权衡。图 9 的 (A) 部分通过绘制公式 10 中的点查询成本 $R$ 与公式 12 中的更新成本 $W$ 而生成。(B) 和 (C) 部分分别对应短范围查询和长范围查询，通过绘制公式 11 中的 $Q$ 与公式 12 中的更新成本 $W$ 而生成，其选择率 $s$ 分别为 $10^{-7}$ 和 $10^{-6}$。空间放大的评估留待实验分析。配置固定为 1TB 数据集、128 字节条目、4KB 存储块，并为全部过滤器总体分配每个条目 10 bit[^5]。我们使用 leveling、tiering 和 Lazy Leveling 各自对应的固定 $K$、$Z$ 值，并改变大小比 $T$，从而生成它们的曲线。圆点表示三种合并策略的收敛点，此处大小比 $T$ 设为 2。方形点表示大小比为 10，这是大多数主流键值存储在实践中默认使用的值 [29, 32]，从而可以比较三个子图中的对应点。图中还展示了标记为 Trans1 和 Trans2 的两条过渡曲线，它们说明Fluid LSM-tree如何在不同设计之间平滑过渡，从而实现使用固定合并策略无法得到的权衡。

**图 9：** 在 Lazy Leveling、leveling 和 tiering 之间平滑切换。

**过渡 1：从 Lazy Leveling 到 Tiering。** 从图 9 的 (A) 部分可以看到，Lazy Leveling 曲线存在一个拐点，越过该点后，减小大小比反而会恶化更新成本。原因是更新成本为 $O((L+T)/B)$；随着 $T$ 减小，$L$ 会增大并开始支配 $T$。在这个例子中，拐点出现在大小比 $T$ 取 5 时。为生成标为过渡 1（Trans1）的曲线，我们将 $T$ 固定为拐点值，转而令 $Z$ 从 1 变化到 $T-1$（本例中为 4）。在该部分设计空间中，所得曲线同时优于 Lazy Leveling 和 tiering，直至与 tiering 收敛。因此，当我们在 Lazy Leveling 与 tiering 之间过渡、分别更多地优化点查询或更新时，过渡 1 能够在点查询成本与更新成本之间取得最优权衡。

**过渡 2：从 Lazy Leveling 到 Leveling。** 为了使 Lazy Leveling 获得更具竞争力的范围查询成本，我们引入过渡 2。其思想是令 $K$（第 1 层至第 $L-1$ 层的 run 数上界）在 1 与 $T-1$ 之间变化，从而在 Lazy Leveling 和 leveling 之间平滑过渡。在图 9 中，我们将 $K$ 固定为 4 并改变 $T$，生成标为 Trans2 的曲线。(A) 部分表明，这使系统能够沿着一条与 Lazy Leveling 类似的权衡曲线移动；(B) 和 (C) 部分表明，Trans2 的范围查询成本与 leveling 几乎相同。因此，过渡 2 能够精细控制系统对短范围查询的优化程度。

**问题：寻找最佳调优配置。** leveling、Lazy Leveling 和 tiering 勾勒出了最优时空权衡空间的边界；通过联合调节参数 $K$、$Z$ 和 $T$，我们还可以到达它们之间的许多其他权衡点。我们的目标是联合调节这些参数，为特定应用取得最佳权衡。挑战在于，这些参数存在许多不同的组合，因此难以找到最优配置[^6]。

[^5]: 我们设置 $N=2^{33}$、$E=2^7$、$B=2^5$，以及 $M=10\cdot2^{33}$。

[^6]: 组合数量为 $O((N/(B\cdot P))^3)$。原因是 $T$ 的最大值为 $T_{lim}=N/(B\cdot P)$，而 $K$ 和 $Z$ 都可以调节为 1 到 $T-1$ 之间的任意值。

sections.title: 4.3 Dostoevsky
5 EVALUATION
sections.originalMarkdown:
We evaluate Dostoevsky across a range of workloads and show that it dominates existing designs in terms of performance and space-amplification.

**Experimental Infrastructure.** We use a machine with a RAID of 500GB 7200RPM disks, 32GB DDR4 main memory, 4 2.7 GHz cores with 8MB L3 caches, running 64-bit Ubuntu 16.04 LTS on an ext4 partition with journaling turned off.

**Implementation.** We implemented Dostoevsky on RocksDB [29], an LSM-tree based key-value store that is widely used in industry [8, 27]. RocksDB only supports leveling and assigns fixed FPR to Bloom filters across all levels. We optimized the Bloom filters allocation by embedding Equation 9 within the code. We then implemented Fluid LSM-tree using a RocksDB API that enables listening to internal events and scheduling merge operations using custom logic. We implemented auto-tuning by measuring the proportion of different operations in the workload during time windows and feeding them to Equation 14. We control the main memory budget by enabling direct IO, and as key-value stores in industry [29, 32] we set the buffer size to 2 MB, the number of bits per entry across all Bloom filters to 10, the density of fence pointers to one per 32KB block, and the block cache size to be 10% of the dataset size in each experiment.

**Metrics.** To measure the latency of updates and lookups, we monitor the amount of time spent by the disk processing lookups and merge operations, respectively, and we divide these measures by the number of lookups and updates in each trial. We measure space-amplification by dividing the raw data size by the volume of unique entries inserted. Each point in the resulting figures represents an average of three experimental trials.

**Baselines.** We compare Dostoevsky to RocksDB [29] and to Monkey [22], which represent the state-of-the-art designs in industry and research, respectively. For RocksDB, we compare against the default tuning (leveling with a size ratio of 10) and against our own tuning of the size ratio to maximize throughput. We experimented with Monkey as a subset of Dostoevsky with only tiering and leveling in its design space. To enable a finer study of the design space, we also compare among five fixed merge policies: Leveling, Tiering, and Lazy Leveling all with a size ratio of 10 and optimized Bloom filters, as well as Transitions 1 and 2 from Section 4.2, which represent combinations of these policies. Write-ahead-logging is enabled across all systems. Since all of these baselines run on top of the RocksDB infrastructure, any variations in performance or space are purely due to differences in data structure design and tuning.

**Experimental Setup.** The default setup involves inserting 1TB of data to an empty database. Every entry is 1KB (the key is 128 bytes and the attached value is 896 bytes, which is common in practice [8]). We compare the designs under worst-case conditions using by default a worst-case workload consisting of uniformly randomly distributed operations, and we vary the proportion of updates and lookups to test the extent to which we can optimize throughput given the contention between their costs. This is in line with the Yahoo Cloud Service Benchmark [21], which is commonly used to evaluate key-value stores today [39, 45, 47, 48, 53]. For generality, we also compare the designs across workload skews. To ensure experimental control, we begin each trial from a fresh clone, as we discuss in more detail in Appendix F.
